title: qs.stats.gini permanent AttributeError — sweep made it observable but didn't fix the dead metric
summary: qs.stats.gini does not exist on the pinned quantstats==0.0.81 — confirmed via `python3 -c 'import quantstats as qs; print(hasattr(qs.stats, "gini"))'` returns False. Every analytics run will hit `metrics_json["gini"] = _safe_float(qs.stats.gini(returns))` and raise AttributeError on every strategy. The sweep wrapped the call in a try/except and emits a WARNING — but the underlying call site is permanently broken. The new test `test_compute_all_metrics_gini_qstats_attribute_missing_logs_warning` documents this as a 'live regression discovered during sweep' but only asserts the WARNING fires; it does NOT fix the call. Operators will see a permanent WARNING on every analytics run that they cannot resolve, and the gini field will be permanently missing. This is the 'fail-loud noise floor' anti-pattern: real future regressions will drown in this permanent warning.
evidence: metrics.py:391 `metrics_json["gini"] = _safe_float(qs.stats.gini(returns))` — qs.stats has no gini attribute on the pinned 0.0.81 version. Verified live. test_metrics.py:1006-1031 documents it as discovered-during-sweep but doesn't fix the call.
fix: Either (a) remove the gini call entirely and drop the metric, (b) implement gini coefficient locally using numpy (single-line formula), or (c) detect attribute-absence at import time with `if not hasattr(qs.stats, 'gini'): logger.info('qs.stats.gini not available on pinned version; skipping') else: ...`. Current state of permanent per-run WARN noise is unacceptable.

title: fetch_mark_prices Binance/Bybit per-row `except (KeyError, TypeError, ValueError): continue` silently drops symbols with zero logging
summary: Inside the per-row mark-price extraction loop for Binance and Bybit, the `try: price = float(row["markPrice"]) except (KeyError, TypeError, ValueError): continue` silently skips rows with malformed/missing markPrice. NO logging at any level. The caller (per docstring at L1365-L1367) decides what to fall back to but cannot distinguish 'symbol not in response' from 'symbol present but malformed markPrice'. This is exactly the silent-failure pattern the sweep targets — same file, same audit window, and the agent did NOT address it. The outer `except Exception: logger.warning(...)` only catches RPC-level failures; per-row drops happen below that surface. A regression in Binance/Bybit ticker schema (column rename, type drift) would manifest as 'some open positions silently treated as flat in equity-curve recompute' with no operator signal.
evidence: exchange.py:1422-1425 — `try: price = float(row["markPrice"]) except (KeyError, TypeError, ValueError): continue`. exchange.py:1443-1446 — identical pattern for Bybit. No logger.debug/warning/info call inside the except branch. Caller fallback documented as 'fall back to entry price or treat as flat' (L1366-L1367) which has substantive downstream impact (valuation, equity curve, drawdown).
fix: Replace the silent `continue` with `except (KeyError, TypeError, ValueError) as exc: logger.warning('fetch_mark_prices %s: dropping row sym=%s — unparseable markPrice=%r: %s', exchange.id, sym, row.get('markPrice'), exc); continue`. Mirrors the pattern the sweep already established at exchange.py:1400-1404 for the OKX per-symbol failure path.

title: OKX bill aggregator silently drops bills with non-digit `ts` — no logging
summary: In fetch_daily_pnl's OKX branch the per-bill loop `if ts_raw and ts_raw.isdigit(): ...` silently drops every bill whose `ts` is empty or non-digit. NO else branch, NO log. A schema drift (OKX returning ISO strings instead of millisecond ints, or leading whitespace, or exponent 'e' notation) would silently lose every bill from that page. The sweep's other timestamp-drop sites (OKX fills at L910, Bybit fills at L1060) log at ERROR before continue. This file (services/exchange.py) is in-scope for this PR and the SAME class of silent-drop pattern the sweep targets, but it was not touched.
evidence: exchange.py:499-503: `ts_raw = bill.get("ts", ""); if ts_raw and ts_raw.isdigit(): dt = datetime.fromtimestamp(int(ts_raw) / 1000, ...)`. No else clause. Compare to L908-L916 OKX fill path which logs ERROR with ts_raw + instId + tradeId before continuing.
fix: Add an else branch: `else: logger.warning('OKX bill dropped: unparseable ts=%r (billId=%s, billType=%s)', ts_raw, bill.get('billId'), bill.get('billType'))` mirroring the OKX/Bybit fill timestamp-drop pattern at L910 / L1060.

title: OKX bills paginator inner exception causes silent partial-truncation with no `partial=true` signal
summary: Inside fetch_daily_pnl's OKX branch the per-page paginator catches Exception, logs WARNING, then `break` — partial daily_pnl is returned but the caller has no way to distinguish 'fetched all data' from 'fetched the first page successfully, page 2 failed, truncated'. Same pattern G12.B.1 eliminated for trades (raise typed error so caller retries). The follow-ups doc (F-1) acknowledges this but classifies it as 'out of scope because not bare-pass'. The pre-existing nature of the bug doesn't change that the file is targeted by this PR and the silent-truncation surface remains.
evidence: exchange.py:432-434 logs and breaks: `logger.warning('OKX bills fetch failed for %s page %d: %s', ...); break`. Same pattern at L467-L469. Return path at L623 simply returns daily_pnl with no `partial=true` flag.
fix: Either (a) thread a `(daily_pnl, partial: bool)` tuple through fetch_daily_pnl with the partial flag set to True when any per-page break happened, or (b) raise a typed `OkxBillsPartialFetchError` mirroring `BinancePerSymbolFetchError` at L36-L52 so the caller can decide retry vs accept-partial. Option (b) is closer to the existing in-file pattern.

title: OKX branch in fetch_daily_pnl lacks per-branch try/except — failure severity skewed vs Binance/Bybit (WARNING) → outer ERROR
summary: Binance and Bybit branches inside fetch_daily_pnl have inner try/except logging at WARNING. The OKX branch (L405-L521) has no equivalent per-branch try, so any failure in OKX bill aggregation, archive fetch, or daily-totals computation escapes to the outer `except Exception as e: logger.error('fetch_daily_pnl failed: %s', str(e))` at L620-L621. Operator dashboards alerting on ERROR for Binance/Bybit will silently miss WARNINGs on Binance/Bybit AND alert on every OKX hiccup. The follow-ups doc (F-2) acknowledges this. Asymmetric severity = silent failure on whichever monitoring side is mismatched.
evidence: exchange.py:404 outer try opens. exchange.py:405 enters OKX branch with no inner try/except. exchange.py:523 (Binance) and L575 (Bybit) have inner try wrappers that log at WARNING. exchange.py:620-621 outer wrapper logs ERROR — so OKX failures cross-report at ERROR while Binance/Bybit cross-report at WARNING.
fix: Wrap the OKX branch in its own try/except that logs at WARNING (mirroring the Binance and Bybit branches). Then the outer wrapper at L620 becomes a true backstop for truly-unanticipated paths.

title: `test_bybit_api_timeout` docstring still encodes pre-sweep silent-swallow contract
summary: Test docstring says 'Bybit timeout is silently caught (the except pass block).' That language pins the OLD pre-sweep contract that the silent-failure sweep is explicitly removing. Although the test body still passes (result == [] is still true), the docstring perpetuates the silent-pass mental model for any new dev reading this test. The sweep added new regression tests in test_exchange.py:TestFetchDailyPnlBybitFailLoud that DO assert WARNING fires; this old test now reads as authoritative documentation of a behavior that has been intentionally changed. Risk: a future /simplify pass could drop the WARNING logging citing 'old test docstring confirms silent-swallow is intentional'.
evidence: test_exchange_harness.py:639: `"""Bybit timeout is silently caught (the except pass block)."""`. Sweep diff replaced `except: pass` with `except Exception as exc: logger.warning("Bybit closed_pnl ...", exc_info=True)` at exchange.py:614-618.
fix: Update the test docstring to describe the post-sweep contract: `"""Bybit timeout is fail-soft: result is empty, a WARNING is logged via the inner-branch handler. The outer fetch_daily_pnl wrapper does NOT escalate to ERROR for this branch."""`. Optionally add a caplog assertion that the new WARNING actually fires here too (defense-in-depth).

title: Bybit ISO-conversion path is NOT actually tested — agent renamed test but the rename moved the gap, not closed it
summary: The test `test_bybit_closed_pnl_item_parse_failure_emits_warning` was renamed by the agent from `test_bybit_iso_conversion_failure` (per the docstring at L2871-L2877). The agent acknowledges the new test triggers parse-loop ValueError via `closedPnl: 'NaN-string'`, NOT the ISO conversion path (which uses `datetime.fromtimestamp(int(ts_raw) / 1000, tz=timezone.utc)` at L611-L613). The agent says 'A separate test for the true ISO-conversion path is a follow-up'. So the original sweep targeting comment at exchange.py:584-587 ('ISO conversion raised on a malformed createdTime') is NOT actually regression-tested. The ISO branch executes AFTER the per-item append loop, so a ValueError from `int(ts_raw)` or `fromtimestamp` is its own failure mode and has zero regression coverage.
evidence: exchange.py:609-613: separate for-loop after the item-parse loop that does ISO conversion. test_exchange.py:2874-2877 acknowledges 'the actual trigger is closedPnl parsing, not the ISO conversion'. The Bybit RPC test (test_bybit_closed_pnl_rpc_failure_emits_warning) and the rename-clarified item-parse test cover the two OTHER failure modes but leave the ISO branch untested.
fix: Add `test_bybit_iso_conversion_failure_emits_warning` that sets `createdTime` to an int-overflow value (e.g. `'99999999999999999999'`) or use `monkeypatch.setattr` on `datetime.fromtimestamp` to raise. Verify the existing WARNING fires and result is still a list.

title: Bybit fail-loud test `assert isinstance(result, list)` is weaker than `result == []` — passes even on partial-success
summary: The Bybit item-parse failure test asserts `isinstance(result, list)` instead of `result == []`. When the per-item parse loop raises on `closedPnl: 'NaN-string'`, the exception happens INSIDE the dict-literal construction (line 601-602 do `float(item.get('closedPnl', 0))` while building the dict). So `daily_pnl.append({...})` never completes for the bad item — but `daily_pnl` itself might contain items appended BEFORE the bad one in a multi-item test. The current test only has one item so happens to produce `[]`, but the assertion `isinstance(result, list)` would PASS even if a regression silently appended partial items. Weak assertion masks partial-failure contracts.
evidence: test_exchange.py:2897-2899: `result = await fetch_daily_pnl(mock_exchange); assert isinstance(result, list)`. The Bybit branch at exchange.py:597-608 appends each item via a dict literal whose values are computed inline — exception during one item exits the entire loop, leaving previously-appended items in daily_pnl.
fix: Tighten the assertion to `assert result == []`. Add a second variant test with multiple items where item-2 raises and item-1 succeeds, then explicitly assert the partial contract (current code returns the partial list — confirm that's intentional).

title: `_safe_float(None)` now emits DEBUG on every legitimate None passthrough — high-volume noise floor when DEBUG enabled
summary: The sweep added DEBUG logging to `_safe_float` for both TypeError/ValueError and NaN/Inf paths. But `_safe_float` is called from `sanitize_metrics` (L205-L222) which recurses through entire metrics dicts, calling `_safe_float` on every numeric value including `None`. `float(None)` raises TypeError, triggering the DEBUG log. Verified live: `_safe_float(None)` produces `DEBUG:quantalyze.analytics.metrics:_safe_float coerce failed (type=NoneType): float() argument must be a string or a real number, not 'NoneType'`. For a strategy with 20 optional scalars where 5 are legitimately None (normal — insufficient data), that's 5 DEBUG logs per analytics run JUST from sanitize_metrics. In production DEBUG is filtered, but if an operator enables DEBUG to triage a real issue, this noise floor will drown the signal they came for — defeating the helper's stated triage purpose.
evidence: metrics.py:197-202 logs DEBUG on every TypeError/ValueError including `float(None)`. metrics.py:205-222 `sanitize_metrics` calls `_safe_float` on every numeric value AND on every list element. Live test: `_safe_float(None)` → DEBUG fires.
fix: Add `if value is None: return None` as the first line of `_safe_float` BEFORE the try block. None is a legitimate normal-path input from sanitize_metrics and qs-returned-None paths; it's not a coerce failure. Reserve the DEBUG log for real type-coercion failures (numpy.complex128, Decimal, etc.) the docstring claims to catch.

title: Convention drift: `# fail-soft:` (new) vs `# best-effort:` (existing) inline comments
summary: The sweep introduces `# fail-soft:` annotations on every wrapped try block. The rest of analytics-service uses `# best-effort:` for the same intent. Two parallel conventions mean a future codebase-wide grep for 'silent-failure audit annotations' will miss half the sites. This is documented as F-4 in the follow-ups doc and explicitly DEFERRED.
evidence: metrics.py L347, L355, L375, L389, L397, L405, L413, L423, L433, L443, L451, L472, L559 — 'fail-soft' annotation prefix. exchange.py L592 also uses 'fail-soft'. Pre-existing convention in analytics-service/ uses 'best-effort'.
fix: Pick one. The follow-ups doc recommends `# best-effort:` since it's the older convention. A one-line sed sweep replacing `# fail-soft:` with `# best-effort:` would close it.

title: `exc_info=True` traceback may leak ccxt-embedded credentials into Railway logs
summary: The sweep added `exc_info=True` to the new WARNING logs in fetch_daily_pnl. ccxt exception classes occasionally embed the full HTTP response (some branches include the request body in `str(exc)`). For Binance/Bybit RPC failures where the request was a signed authenticated call, the traceback may include the signed-request URL or headers in the exception args. Documented as F-3 in the follow-ups doc; the project has no `redact_secrets` utility.
evidence: exchange.py:552-562 — `logger.warning('Binance futures-income failed ...', exc, exc_info=True)`. exchange.py:614-618 — `logger.warning('Bybit closed_pnl fetch ...', exc, exc_info=True)`. ccxt exceptions can embed response bodies via the `__cause__` chain.
fix: Either (a) drop `exc_info=True` from these two WARNINGs and rely on the message text (loses traceback), or (b) introduce `services/log_redact.py` with a regex-based secret scrubber and wrap the WARNINGs. Option (b) is a defense-in-depth utility the codebase needs anyway.

title: `test_compute_all_metrics_qstats_scalar_warnings_carry_exc_info` only tests ONE scalar (var_1d_95) — pins exc_info contract for 1/11 sweep sites
summary: The exc_info=True regression test only verifies var_1d_95. The sweep added exc_info=True to 11 WARNING call sites in metrics.py. A regression that drops exc_info=True on any of the other 10 sites (cvar/omega/gain_pain/tail_ratio/smart_sharpe/smart_sortino/profit_factor/var_1m_99/skewness/kurtosis) would NOT be caught. The test self-documents this as 'pins it for one representative scalar' but representative isn't comprehensive.
evidence: test_metrics.py:1190-1215 only monkeypatches qs.stats.value_at_risk and asserts var_1d_95 WARNING has exc_info. Outlier ratios (L1086-1094), Bybit closed_pnl (L2853-2860), Binance futures-income (L2986-2988) DO assert exc_info for their respective sites. So 4/11 sites are covered, 7 are not.
fix: Extend the parametrized test at L958-L1003 to ALSO assert exc_info is not None for every parametrized scalar (one extra assertion line in the same loop). Then the 8 qs.stats scalars are covered; outlier-ratios and Bybit/Binance are already covered separately.

title: skew/kurtosis tests monkeypatch pd.Series globally — may interfere with parallel test execution
summary: The new tests monkeypatch `pd.Series.skew`, `pd.Series.kurtosis`, and `pd.Series.mean` (the outlier test) at the class level via `monkeypatch.setattr(pd.Series, 'skew', boom)`. pytest's monkeypatch fixture undoes this at test teardown, but if pytest-xdist runs these tests in parallel with other tests in the same worker, they may interfere with concurrent tests calling `pd.Series.skew()`. Less of a 'silent failure' and more of a 'test flakiness' concern, but flake hides regressions just as effectively as silence.
evidence: test_metrics.py:1143 `monkeypatch.setattr(pd.Series, 'skew', boom)` — affects every Series in the process. The outlier-ratios test at L1051-L1095 takes precautions by checking `str(self.dtype) == 'bool'` before raising, but the skew/kurtosis variants do not.
fix: Use the same dtype-conditional pattern from the outlier-ratios test for skew/kurtosis: raise only when the series matches the strategy-returns series (e.g., `if len(self) > 100`). Or use `unittest.mock.patch.object` on a specific instance.
